activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze how dialogue and story structure advance the plot and reveal key developments in Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Your Turn" segment of the Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry Story Structure Skill lesson. It provides a focused sequence that guides students through analyzing how specific dialogue and structural elements contribute to plot development and meaning.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the end-of-unit argumentative essay "Can relationships shape your future?" by developing students' ability to analyze how authors structure scenes to show relationships changing over time. Students practice identifying how dialogue moves plot forward and how structural elements (like silence) mark turning points - skills they'll need when analyzing how relationships develop and transform characters throughout the texts in this unit.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This task directly connects to the essential question "How do relationships shape us?" by examining how economic pressure and intimidation are breaking apart the solidarity between the Logan family and community members like Mr. Avery. Students analyze the structural techniques authors use to show relationships under strain, helping them understand how external forces can reshape human connections and community bonds.
activity_metadata.text_title: Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Excerpt from Chapter 9 of Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry
Paragraphs 22-26

(22) "You goin' up to the store tomorrow, David?" Mr. Avery asked after all the amenities had been said. Since the first trip in January, Mr. Morrison had made one other trip to Vicksburg, but Papa had not gone with him.

(23) Papa motioned to Mr. Morrison. "Mr. Morrison and me going the day after tomorrow. Your wife brought down that list of things you need yesterday."

(24) Mr. Avery cleared his throat nervously. "It's—it's that list I come 'bout, David. ...I don't want them things no more."

(25) The porch grew silent.

(26) When no one said anything, Mr. Avery glanced at Mr. Lanier, and Mr. Lanier shook his head and continued. "Mr. Granger making it hard on us, David. Said we gonna have to give him sixty percent of the cotton, 'stead of fifty ...now that the cotton's planted and it's too late to plant more. ...Don't s'pose though that it makes much difference. The way cotton sells these days, seems the more we plant, the less money we gets anyways—"
exit_ticket: Which of the following statements best summarizes how the dialogue in this passage moves the plot forward?
Which sentence from the passage supports your answer to the previous question?
